title: Psalms 14
subtitle: The Fool Says There Is No God

heb.paragraph: "The fool (nābāl) says in his heart, “There is no God”" (v.1). Nābāl is not intellectual stupidity but moral bankruptcy — the same word for Nabal (1 Sam 25). The fool denies God not from philosophy but from lifestyle: living as if God does not see or judge.

refs.note: Paul quotes Psalm 14:1–3 for universal depravity: "there is no one righteous, not even one."
"Every inclination of the thoughts of the human heart was only evil" — the flood narrative’s identical assessment.

notes.note: "The fool says ‘There is no God.’" Not intellectual atheism but practical godlessness. Living without reference to God.
"The LORD looks down from heaven... all have turned away." The divine survey finds universal corruption. Paul builds Romans 3 on this.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "in his heart" means this is an inner conviction, not a public declaration. The fool’s atheism is private and practical.
Calvin: the fool denies not God’s existence but God’s relevance. Calvin: practical atheism — living as if God does not intervene — is more common than theoretical atheism.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: nābāl describes someone who is morally senseless, not intellectually deficient. The parallel in 1 Samuel 25 (Nabal) confirms the moral reading.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: nābāl’s folly is moral, not intellectual. The "there is no God" is not a philosophical proposition but a lifestyle assumption.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the psalm provides the theological basis for Paul’s argument in Romans 3. Universal corruption is not a NT invention but an OT observation.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "not even one" is absolute. The divine survey is comprehensive and the result is total. No exceptions from below; grace must come from above.

hist.context: God looks down from heaven to see if anyone understands or seeks him (v.2). The verdict: all have turned away, all are corrupt, no one does good — not even one (v.3). Paul quotes this in Romans 3:10–12 for his universal sinfulness argument. The "evildoers" devour God’s people like bread (v.4).

sections.header: Verses 5–7 — “Oh, That Salvation Would Come from Zion!”

heb.paragraph: "Oh, that salvation (yəshūʿâ) for Israel would come out of Zion!" (v.7). The psalm ends with longing for corporate deliverance. When God restores his people, Jacob will rejoice. The hope is national, messianic, and eschatological.

refs.note: "The deliverer will come from Zion" — Paul applies this verse to Christ’s eschatological salvation.
Psalm 53 is a near-duplicate of Psalm 14 with minor variations — both traditions preserved.

notes.note: "God is present in the company of the righteous." Despite the universal corruption diagnosis, God maintains a faithful remnant.
"Oh, that salvation would come from Zion!" The longing for messianic deliverance. When God restores, all Israel rejoices.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the final exclamation shifts from observation to desire. The psalm moves from diagnostic (vv.1–4) to hopeful (v.7).
Calvin: salvation FROM Zion is Christ. Calvin: the psalm’s survey of human corruption makes the need for a divine saviour inescapable.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "when the LORD restores his people" uses the phrase shūb shəbūṭ — "turn the turning," i.e., reverse the captivity. The same phrase describes return from exile.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the longing for Zion’s salvation transcends David’s situation. This is messianic hope: a deliverer FROM Zion for all God’s people.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the remnant theology (v.5) alongside universal corruption (v.3) is the OT pattern: all are fallen, but God preserves a faithful community.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "Oh, that!" (mî-yittēn) is a wish, not a demand. The psalm ends in longing, not possession. Salvation is hoped for, not yet realised.

hist.context: Despite universal corruption, God is present with the righteous generation (v.5). The wicked are overwhelmed with dread because "God is present in the company of the righteous" (v.5). The psalm closes with a longing: "Oh, that salvation would come from Zion!" (v.7). The cry for a saviour from Zion anticipates Christ.

ppl.name: God (Yahweh)
ppl.role: The divine judge who searches humanity from heaven (vv.2-3)
ppl.text: God 'looks down from heaven' (v.2) — the same verb (hashqiph) used of God surveying Sodom (Gen 18:16) and Egypt on Passover night (Exod 14:24). His verdict is total: 'there is no one who does good, not even one' (v.3). Paul quotes this passage in Romans 3:10-12 as the capstone of his argument for universal human sinfulness, making Psalm 14 foundational to the doctrine of total depravity.

ppl.name: The Fool (nabal)
ppl.role: The one who denies God's relevance (v.1)
ppl.text: The nabal is not an intellectual atheist but a practical one — the word connotes moral bankruptcy rather than philosophical doubt (cf. Nabal in 1 Sam 25). His denial 'in his heart' is volitional, not cognitive. The fool's corruption produces 'abominable deeds' (ta'alilah), a term elsewhere associated with idolatry and covenant treachery.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Attributed author; king confronting systemic injustice
ppl.text: David observes rulers who 'devour my people as they eat bread' (v.4) — a metaphor for economic exploitation that treats vulnerable Israelites as consumable resources. This social critique anticipates the prophetic tradition. The psalm is nearly identical to Psalm 53, with the key difference being the divine name: Psalm 14 uses Yahweh (covenant name), Psalm 53 uses Elohim (universal name).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVOh, that salvation for Israel would come out of Zion! When the Lord restores his people, let Jacob rejoice and Israel be glad!ESVOh, that salvation for Israel would come out of Zion! When the LORD restores the fortunes of his people, let Jacob rejoice, let Israel be glad.NIVBut there they are, overwhelmed with dread, for God is present in the company of the righteous.ESVThere they are in great terror, for God is with the generation of the righteous.

rec.text: ""The fool says ‘There is no God.’" Not intellectual atheism but practical godlessness. Living without reference to God."

rows.label: Zion’s Hope — 14:5–7
rows.text: "Oh, that salvation would come from Zion!" God with the righteous. Messianic longing.

hebtext.note: "The fool (nābāl) says in his heart, “There is no God”" (v.1). Nābāl is not intellectual stupidity but moral bankruptcy — the same word for Nabal (1 Sam 25). The fool denies God not from philosophy but from lifestyle: living as if God does not see or judge.

"Oh, that salvation (yəshūʿâ) for Israel would come out of Zion!" (v.7). The psalm ends with longing for corporate deliverance. When God restores his people, Jacob will rejoice. The hope is national, messianic, and eschatological.

themes.note: Prophecy: universal corruption anticipates Romans 3; Zion’s salvation anticipates Christ. Judgment: the divine survey finds none righteous. Justice: the fool’s denial meets God’s presence.
